coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of H2S in the environment with electrochemiluminescence and fluorescence double-mode sensor constructed by Eu2O3@CDs NPs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Chunyuan
Tang, Feiyan
Wang, Mingxia
Zhou, Jie
Yue, Xidian
Luan, Feng
Zhuang, Xuming
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an eco-friendly and rapid dual-mode detection method for H2S based on europium oxide doped with carbon quantum dots nanocomposites (Eu2O3@CDs NPs) is proposed. An efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor and dual emission ratio type fluorescence (FL) sensor was constructed by Eu2O3@CDs NPs, which exhibit excellent ECL and FL properties. It successfully realized the precise quantitative detection of H2S due to the influence of the sulfion (S2-) to resonance energy transfer mechanism in Eu2O3@CDs NPs and the extremely short response time greatly improves the analysis efficiency. Ultimately, a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 1 pM to 10 mM and 50 nM to 0.5 mM of H2S was acquired, respectively, and the detection limit was as low as 0.0537 pM and 4.88 nM (S/N = 3). In addition, both sensors show excellent selectivity, and stability, and provide a novel idea for establishing H2S sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: H2S
Electrochemiluminescence
Fluorescence
Eu2O3@CDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Constructions and measurements of ECL, dual-emission ratiometric FL sensors
The performance of the dual-mode sensor
Conditional optimization of the dual-mode sensor
The mechanism of the dual-mode sensor
The performance of the dual-mode sensor
Conditional optimization of the dual-mode sensor
The mechanism of the dual-mode sensor
Linearity of the dual-mode sensor for S2- detection
Selectivity and stability of the dual-mode sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2023MB047, ZR2021MB024).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM images of Eu(OH)3 and (B) Eu2O3@CDs, respectively. (C) The XRD patterns of Eu(OH)3 and Eu2O3. (D) The FT-IR spectrum of the Eu2O3 and Eu2O3@CDs.
(A) Eu2O3@CDs ECL spectrum by the filter of different wavelengths, (B) CDs, Eu2O3, Eu2O3@CDs FL spectrum in Ex = 360 nm.

(A) The ECL response of the sensor at different S2- concentrations (0–10 mM). (B) Linear relationship and equation of the ECL sensor for S2- determination (1 pM-10 mM). (C) The selectivity of the ECL sensor for the same concentration of 1 mM interference ions and adding S2- in extra. (D) ECL stability of the Eu2O3@CDs/GCE in K2S2O8 solution (0.1 M, pH=7.4) in 20 circles.

(A) Dual-emission FL response of the sensor at different S2- concentrations of 0–0.5 mM (The illustration was the illumination diagram of the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp). (B) Linear relationship and equation of the dual-emission ratio FL sensor for S2- detection (50 nM-0.5 mM). (C) The selectivity of the FL sensor for the same concentration of 1 mM of interference ions and S2- (The illustration was the illumination diagram of the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp). (D) FL stability of the Eu2O3@CDs sensing platform in 7 days.
Determination of S2- in actual environment samples by ECL sensor (n = 3).
Determination of S2- in actual environment samples by FL sensor (n=3).

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of H2S in the environment with electrochemiluminescence and fluorescence double-mode sensor constructed by Eu2O3@CDs NPs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai, Shandong 264005, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai, Shandong 264005, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Yantai University, Yantai, Shandong 264005, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Yantai University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Yantai University, Yantai, Shandong 264005, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an eco-friendly and rapid dual-mode detection method for H2S based on europium oxide doped with carbon quantum dots nanocomposites (Eu2O3@CDs NPs) is proposed. An efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor and dual emission ratio type fluorescence (FL) sensor was constructed by Eu2O3@CDs NPs, which exhibit excellent ECL and FL properties. It successfully realized the precise quantitative detection of H2S due to the influence of the sulfion (S2-) to resonance energy transfer mechanism in Eu2O3@CDs NPs and the extremely short response time greatly improves the analysis efficiency. Ultimately, a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 1 pM to 10 mM and 50 nM to 0.5 mM of H2S was acquired, respectively, and the detection limit was as low as 0.0537 pM and 4.88 nM (S/N = 3). In addition, both sensors show excellent selectivity, and stability, and provide a novel idea for establishing H2S sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is a harmful and polluting gas; its accumulation in the air at certain concentrations can lead to acid rain, posing a significant threat to the ecological environment and human health. [1,2]. Moreover, the S2- in H2S has a powerful coordination effect on heavy metal ions in the environment such as Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe2+ [3–5]. The resulting sulfides decompose slowly and persist in nature for a long time. Seriously, the accumulation of the food chain can induce diseases and endanger life in the human body [6,7]. Therefore, the real-time monitoring of the presence and concentration of H2S in the environment is an urgent problem that researchers have been solving in recent years, and the development of various new H2S sensors with high sensitivity and fast response has also become a research hotspot.

Surface-enhanced Raman scattering [8], colorimetry [9], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometric [10], and gas sensing methods have been widely utilized in H2S detection [11]. However, complicated operations and expensive instruments make the development of economic and easy-to-operate new sensors more attractive. In recent years, fluorescence (FL) analysis has been considered one of the most promising rapid optical methods [12,13]. The material with the luminous group can emit light of the characteristic wavelength through the irradiation of certain light. The analysis results of visualization can be implemented when the changes in the radiation wavelength are due to the existence of the target [14,15]. It is widely used in medical fields such as biological imaging [16], clinical diagnosis [17], medical treatment [18], genetic analysis [19], etc. Many fluorescent probes have been developed for the detection of H2S, including the following strategies: (1) Reduction of azide/nitro by H2S [20], (2) H2S addition reaction to unsaturated bond [21], (3) complex formation of H2S with metal ion [22], aromatic nucleophilic substitution [23]. However, large background interference inevitably exists in that light is both the source of excitation and the output signal in FL sensors [24,25]. In contrast, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor is a new and efficient electrochemical (EC) detection method with a higher signal-noise ratio and lower limit of detection (LOD) [26,27], which has been widely used in biological immunoassay [28], environmental monitoring [29], drug analysis [30], and other fields. The key to establishing an efficient ECL sensor is to synthesize an electroactive material with excellent electron mobility and optical properties that can generate high-energy excited states by colliding with co-reactive ions under a certain voltage [31,32]. However, the single-mode inspection technology is considered to have relatively poor stability to an analytical result, ascribed to the disturbance of the instrument itself, environment, operation factor, and so on [33]. Thus, increasing scientific research and exploration tend to build a double-mode mechanism to accurately determine the existing target, to achieve the self-verification of the output signal. Dual-emission carbon nanodots (CDs) were designed to combine FL and colorimetric technology for the competent detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in lake water by the research team of Shen [34]. The synthesized gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) were developed to construct FL and EC sensors for a dual-mode, label-free strategy, and the supersensitive response of poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 (PARP-1) of the cancer cell expression was successfully achieved by Xu [35]. Wei et al. were doped ruthenium-based metal-organic framework with lead ion a (Pb2+-Ru MOF), combined with ECL and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method to obtain a more reliable response to Vibrio parahaemolyticus (VP) [36]. Based on this, for access to analysis results with high accuracy, high sensitivity, and strong stability, the simple ECL and FL double-sensing platforms were designed to rapidly respond to potential H2S in the environment.

In recent years, as a small carbon-based nanomaterial with a core-shell structure, carbon dots (CDs) have the characteristics of low cost, high stability, adjustable optical properties, and good biocompatibility [37,38]. Therefore, the modification and doping of CDs and the structure of relevant sensors with multi-function have become a research hotspot [39,40]. Besides, rare earth elements are widely used in the development of fluorescent probes due to their rich emission bands and excellent fluorescence lifetime [41,42]. Eu3+ has become the most promising rare earth luminescent material due to its intense luminescence in the red spectral region and high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) [43,44]. In recent years, it has been found that rare earth elements also perform well in the field of ECL, and the corresponding semiconductor nanomaterials have been developed and explored as a new excellent ECL luminous group. It was previously reported that europium sulfide nanocrystals (EuS NCs) were utilized to build the human immune deficiency virus (HIV) ECL sensing platform and fulfilled the ultrasensitive determination of HIV DNA by the Du team [45]. A cathodic ECL signal amplification strategy was designed with the europium oxide (Eu2O3) doped with graphite-like carbonitride (Eu2O3/g-C3N4) and estradiol (E2) realized sensitive detection in water, in which the presence of oxygen vacancy improved catalytic activity [46]. A biosensor probe based on ligand energy transfer Eu metal-organic framework (Eu-MOFs) self-enhanced ECL signal emitter was developed for rapid response to ulinastatin, which proved the superiority and potential of rare earth elements in the field of ECL sensors in the report [47].

Herein, an environmentally friendly one-pot doped quantum dots nanocomposite (Eu2O3@CD NPs) was synthesized and exhibited, exhibiting excellent optical and electrical properties. Therefore, the cathode ECL and dual-ratio FL dual-mode sensing platform were simultaneously structured based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect formed by CDs acting as electron donor Eu2O3 as receptor. According to a sequence of experimental research [48], the FRET structure and efficiency diminished in the presence of S2- to chelate Eu3+, which can achieve accurate monitoring of H2S in the environment. This strategy provided an innovation for the sensitive detection of more environmental pollutants and proposed broader prospects for the development and utilization of rare-earth-based nanomaterials in the frontier field.

Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3∙6 H2O, 99.9 %), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (HTAB, ≥99 %), and citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5 %) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8, AR) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S∙6 H2O, ≥98 %) was obtained from Shanghai Tongya Chemical Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, AR), and disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4, AR) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (AR) was obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). L-Glutathione reduced (GSH, ≥98 %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). The lake water was taken from Sanyuan Lake of Yantai University, tap water was directly accessed from the water supply pipeline of Yantai University, and the fish was bought from Yantai Market (Yantai, China). All other reagents are of AR without any purification operation before use unless otherwise specified. During the whole experiment, ultrapure water was used (18.25 MΩ cm).

All the response signals of FL were obtained from the output of the F-4700 FL Photometer of Hitachi Hi-Tech Science (Japan). The ECL test data were measured by the type of MPI-E ECL AMPI-E ECL Analysis System type rochemical Analyzer and Multifunctional Chemiluminescence detector (Xian, China). The Puxi TU-1901 Double-beam Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) Spectrophotometer (Beijing, China) acquired the absorbance spectrogram. The UV lamp images were obtained by the JY02S UV analyzer of Junyi Dongfang Electrophoresis Equipment Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) element mapping images were got by the JSM-7900 F (Japan), and the transmission electron microscope (TEM) diagram was prepared by JEM-2100 F Japan Electronics Co., Ltd (Japan). X-ray diffractometer (XRD) spectrum and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was tested by XRD- Smart Lab (3 kW, Japan), and IR Affinity-1S (China), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was completed by ESCALAB-250 Thermo Fisher Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Firstly, the europium hydroxide (Eu(OH)3) was fabricated following the previous work and made some changes [49], Na2S∙6 H2O (4.6 g, 10 mL) aqueous solution was slowly added to 1.1 g Eu(NO3)3∙6 H2O solution (1.1 g, 10 mL) which was heated and kept at constant to 90 ℃, stirred and refluxed 1 h. The products were washed with ultrapure water and ethanol three times respectively and dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h to obtain the white powder of Eu(OH)3, which was stored in the refrigerator at 4 ℃ for future use.

0.2000 g Eu(OH)3 of the above synthesized, 1.000 g CA, 0.1700 g glutathione, and 0.0380 g HTAB were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water together. After being fully dissolved, they were placed in a 45 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and reacted for 3 h under the heating condition of 200℃. The brown-yellow solution acquired was filtered off the black residue with a 0.22 µm organic phase needle filter. Subsequently, it was obvious that white flocs Eu2O3 @CDs NPs were separated along with the 10 mL ethanol solution adding. After centrifugation and washing with ethanol three times, the produce was placed in a drying oven at 60 ℃ overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Constructions and measurements of ECL, dual-emission ratiometric FL sensors
ce:section.ce:para: 5 µL uniformly dispersed Eu2O3@CDs NPs (1 mg, 1 mL) solution was dropped onto the clean glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface polished with Al2O3 powder. The Eu2O3@CDs /GCE ECL sensing platform obtained serves as the working electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode, and Ag /AgCl electrode as the reference electrode to jointly realize the transmission of electrical signals. The K2S2O8 (0.1 M, pH=7.4) as the co-reagent was prepared from 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS), which was provided by the voltage range was set as - 2–0 V, the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set as 800 V, and the scanning speed was set as 0.1 V/s. Add 100 µL S2- with different concentrations into the ECL sensor before collecting and recording the detected signal.

All FL intensity tests were carried out under the same conditions. The excitation and emission slit width was 10 nm and the PMT of 400 V, the excitation wavelength (Ex) was set to 360 nm, and the emission wavelength (Em) range was 390–640 nm. Add uniformly disperse Eu2O3@CDs NPs (2 mg, 3 mL mixed solution (ethanol: water (V:V = 2:1)) into the quartz cuvette. Subsequently, the output signals of the dual-emission ratio FL sensor (Em = 421 nm and 619 nm) were recorded after adding 100 µL of different concentrations S2- respectively and calculated the FL intensity ratio (F421 nm/F619 nm) to establish the corresponding linear relationship.

To evaluate the applicability of the dual-mode sensor for the trace detection of H2S concentration in the actual environment, the standard addition method was utilized to simulate the existing state of H2S gas after being absorbed by water. Primarily, take and pretreat 1.0 g of three kinds of environmental samples lake water, tap water, and fish meat. The lake water and tap water were simply processed by filtering, the fish meat though washed and sheared was added with 1 mL of ultrapure water for ultrasonic crushing, ensuing generating filter residue was filtered and discarded. Lastly, the sample solution was prepared into spiked solutions containing S2- with different concentrations, respectively, and the spiked recovery rate of the actual sample was tested.

First, we synthesized Eu(OH)3. TEM images ( Fig. 1A) showed that the Eu(OH)3 is entirely composed of nanorods with an approximate length of 100–120 nm and a diameter of 12 nm. Eu2O3@CDs nanocomposites were obtained through One-pot synthesis method, and the morphology of Eu2O3@CDs changed obviously. The Eu2O3@CDs have small particle sizes and amorphous structures as shown in Fig. 1B. The main elements Eu, O, and C were distributed in the EDS Mapping image of Fig. S1, it was obviously observed that an excellent combination of the two materials Eu2O3 and CDs, in which element C was uniformly doped on the surface of Eu2O3. To further verify the successful synthesis of Eu(OH)3 and Eu2O3, the XRD patterns were compared with the corresponding standard cards for the coincidence of diffraction peaks. It was found that the coincidence could be shown in Fig. 1(C) indicating the successful preparation of the material. The groups on the surface of the synthesized CDs were explored by the FT-IR spectrum as shown in Fig. 1(D), in which the "CO", "C-O" and other groups were identified, and also evidenced the successful modification of CDs. In addition, to further confirm the existence of the valence state of the Eu element in the composite, the diffraction peak at the binding energy of 1135.6 eV at the main peak position is consistent with the standard binding energy of Eu2O3 through fine XPS spectrum analysis in Fig. S2(B). And the Eu2O3@CDs NPs were successfully prepared by the full spectrum analysis of Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore what kind of energy light was emitted in the process of ECL electro-optic conversion, the filter with a specific wavelength (400–700 nm) was added to the constructed ECL sensor. The experimental results were shown in Fig. 2(A) that the ECL performance of the sensor was the best under the intervention of the filter with the wavelength of 440 nm and 660 nm respectively, and both of them were also the main source of the ECL signal output. At the same time, the FL properties of CDs, Eu2O3, and Eu2O3@CDs were compared at the Ex of 360 nm as shown in Fig. 2(B). It can be observed that the FL intensity weakened of Eu2O3@CDs NPs compound material at Em of 421 nm resulting from CDs, and the FL intensity at Em of 592 nm and 619 nm were significantly enhanced from Eu2O3, which showed excellent luminescent characteristics of both blue and red FL emission. By comparison, the emission peak position of the ECL spectrum has a certain degree of red shift compared with the FL spectrum in Eu2O3@CDs NPs, which was generated by the energy loss caused by the collision between the co-reactant molecule and the luminescent material and the electron transfer during the ECL reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditional optimization of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The several experimental conditions of the constructed ECL sensor were optimized. 5 µL 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 mg mL−1 Eu2O3@CDs dispersions were selected for drip coating on the electrode surface. The effect of different drip concentrations on material properties and ECL signal intensity was shown in Fig. S3(A), the excessive accumulation of materials on the electrode surface hinders the transmission of surface electrons and weakens the ECL signal, and the optimal concentration was 1 mg mL−1. In addition, the pH value of co-reactant K2S2O8 in the test process was optimized to sufficiently display the best ECL performance of Eu2O3@CDs/GCE in Fig. S3(A), the acidic environment has led to the destruction of the metal oxide structure, and the presence of excessive OH− in the system had also inhibited the properties of acidic CDs. Therefore, the neutral environment of pH = 7.4 was considered to be the most appropriate condition for the ECL sensing platform. Subsequently, the Ex, the concentration and the solvent ratio of the FL probe, and the reaction time of S2- were optimized respectively, to make the best luminescence performance of the FL sensing platform. First of all, the most optimal Ex was revealed at 360 nm when the range was 330–390 nm as shown in Fig. S4(A). The FL output signal was the strongest as displayed when the concentration of Eu2O3@CDs was 0.677 mg mL−1 in Fig. S4(B). However, in the FL test, water-soluble CDs showed a violent FL signal in the water environment to cover the FL intensity of Eu2O3. Thus, in an effective dual-emission ratio FL sensor platform was built with the mixed solution of ethanol and water selected as the dispersant of Eu2O3@CDs, the most beneficial volume ratio of ethanol and water was 2:1 in Fig. S4(A). After adding the solution to be measured containing target S2- into the FL sensor, and recording the change of FL intensity at different response times as shown in Fig. S4(D), it was found that the FL intensity tends to be stable after 90 seconds of reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The identification mechanism of the S2- to ECL sensor was further described through theory and experimental phenomena. On the one hand, it was observed that the performance of ECL mainly contributes from the light of the characteristic emission wavelength of 660 nm of Eu2O3 in Fig. 2(A), when S2- was present in the environment, the structure of the Eu2O3@CDs was destroyed in virtue of a strong binding effect between S2- and Eu2O3, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of excited states of Eu2O3@CDs ((Eu2O3@CDs)*), which ultimately leads to the quenching of ECL signal. The molecular formula mechanism is as follows:
(1) Eu2O3@CDs+e-→ (Eu2O3@CDs)-•
(2) S2O8 2-+e-→ SO4 2-+ SO4 -•
(3) (Eu2O3@CDs)-•+SO4 -•→ (Eu2O3@CDs)*+ SO4 2-
(4) (Eu2O3@CDs)*→ (Eu2O3@CDs) + hν

To verify the response mechanism of the dual-emission ratio FL sensor, UV-Vis spectrophotometry was utilized to test the absorbance of Eu2O3@CDs. Compared with the emission spectrum of CDs, it was discovered that the absorption peak of Eu2O3@CDs overlaps with the emission peak of CDs in Fig. S5. In addition, the distance between the two materials was enough tiny, which proved that when the composite was excited, the FRET effect of CDs as electron donors and Eu2O3 as electron acceptors can be generated, corresponding FL status was “ON”. This also explained that in the FL contrast diagram as Fig. 2(B), the FL signal of CDs was quenched with the FL sensitized after forming the composites. In the presence of the S2-, the FL peak intensity of CDs recovered while the FL signal of Eu2O3 declined significantly on account of this energy transfer effect destroyed, and the FL state was “OFF”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore what kind of energy light was emitted in the process of ECL electro-optic conversion, the filter with a specific wavelength (400–700 nm) was added to the constructed ECL sensor. The experimental results were shown in Fig. 2(A) that the ECL performance of the sensor was the best under the intervention of the filter with the wavelength of 440 nm and 660 nm respectively, and both of them were also the main source of the ECL signal output. At the same time, the FL properties of CDs, Eu2O3, and Eu2O3@CDs were compared at the Ex of 360 nm as shown in Fig. 2(B). It can be observed that the FL intensity weakened of Eu2O3@CDs NPs compound material at Em of 421 nm resulting from CDs, and the FL intensity at Em of 592 nm and 619 nm were significantly enhanced from Eu2O3, which showed excellent luminescent characteristics of both blue and red FL emission. By comparison, the emission peak position of the ECL spectrum has a certain degree of red shift compared with the FL spectrum in Eu2O3@CDs NPs, which was generated by the energy loss caused by the collision between the co-reactant molecule and the luminescent material and the electron transfer during the ECL reaction

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditional optimization of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The several experimental conditions of the constructed ECL sensor were optimized. 5 µL 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 mg mL−1 Eu2O3@CDs dispersions were selected for drip coating on the electrode surface. The effect of different drip concentrations on material properties and ECL signal intensity was shown in Fig. S3(A), the excessive accumulation of materials on the electrode surface hinders the transmission of surface electrons and weakens the ECL signal, and the optimal concentration was 1 mg mL−1. In addition, the pH value of co-reactant K2S2O8 in the test process was optimized to sufficiently display the best ECL performance of Eu2O3@CDs/GCE in Fig. S3(A), the acidic environment has led to the destruction of the metal oxide structure, and the presence of excessive OH− in the system had also inhibited the properties of acidic CDs. Therefore, the neutral environment of pH = 7.4 was considered to be the most appropriate condition for the ECL sensing platform. Subsequently, the Ex, the concentration and the solvent ratio of the FL probe, and the reaction time of S2- were optimized respectively, to make the best luminescence performance of the FL sensing platform. First of all, the most optimal Ex was revealed at 360 nm when the range was 330–390 nm as shown in Fig. S4(A). The FL output signal was the strongest as displayed when the concentration of Eu2O3-CDs was 0.677 mg mL−1 in Fig. S4(B). However, in the FL test, water-soluble CDs showed a violent FL signal in the water environment to cover the FL intensity of Eu2O3. Thus, in an effective dual-emission ratio FL sensor platform was built with the mixed solution of ethanol and water selected as the dispersant of Eu2O3@CDs, the most beneficial volume ratio of ethanol and water was 2:1 in Fig. S4(A). After adding the solution to be measured containing target S2- into the FL sensor, and recording the change of FL intensity at different response times as shown in Fig. S4(D), it was found that the FL intensity tends to be stable after 90 seconds of reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The identification mechanism of the S2- to ECL sensor was further described through theory and experimental phenomena. On the one hand, it was observed that the performance of ECL mainly contributes from the light of the characteristic emission wavelength of 660 nm of Eu2O3 in Fig. 2(A), when S2- was present in the environment, the structure of the Eu2O3@CDs was destroyed in virtue of a strong binding effect between S2- and Eu2O3, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of excited states of Eu2O3@CDs ((Eu2O3@CDs)*), which ultimately leads to the quenching of ECL signal. The molecular formula mechanism is as follows:
Eu2O3@CDs+e-→ (Eu2O3@CDs)-•(1)
S2O8 2-+e-→SO4 2-+SO4 -•(2)
(Eu2O3@CDs)-•+SO4 -•→ (Eu2O3@CDs) *+SO4 2-(3)
(Eu2O3@CDs)*→ (Eu2O3@CDs) + hν(4)

To verify the response mechanism of the dual-emission ratio FL sensor, UV-Vis spectrophotometry was utilized to test the absorbance of Eu2O3@CDs. Compared with the emission spectrum of CDs, it was discovered that the absorption peak of Eu2O3@CDs overlaps with the emission peak of CDs in Fig. S5. In addition, the distance between the two materials was enough tiny, which proved that when the composite was excited, the FRET effect of CDs as electron donors and Eu2O3 as electron acceptors can be generated, corresponding FL status was “ON”. This also explained that in the FL contrast diagram as Fig. 2(B), the FL signal of CDs was quenched with the FL sensitized after forming the composites. In the presence of the S2-, the FL peak intensity of CDs recovered while the FL signal of Eu2O3 declined significantly on account of this energy transfer effect destroyed, and the FL state was “OFF”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linearity of the dual-mode sensor for S2- detection
ce:section.ce:para: The constructed ECL sensing platform was applied to the detection of S2- at different concentrations (1 pM - 10 mM), and the relationship between concentration gradient and ECL signal strength was obtained as shown in Fig. 3(A), in there the signal value decreased with the increase of S2- concentration, which was consistent with the experimental quenching mechanism. The quantitative relationship between S2- concentration and ECL sensing signal change value ΔI (ΔII0-I, I0 was the signal value of the blank solution, I was the signal value measured at different concentrations of S2-) was explored as shown in Fig. 3(B). The linear fitting equation was ΔI=1008 lgc + 1827.4 (R2=0.9971), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.0537 pM (S/N = 3). Similarly, the dual-emission FL sensor was applied to specifically respond to S2- in a certain concentration range from 50 nM to 0.5 mM in Fig. 4(A), with the concentration of the target molecule, the FL peak signal amplified of Em at 421 nm, and the intensity diminished of Em at 619 nm by contrast, and the concentration of S2- was added to 3 mL of fluorescent probe solution counterclockwise from low to high under the irradiation of the 365 nm ultraviolet analyzer at the bottom right corner in inner illustration, and the color gradually turns from purple to blue. Besides, the quantitative relationship between S2- concentration and FL signal intensity ratio (F419 nm/F621 nm) was searched in Fig. 4(B). The linear fitting equation was F419 nm/F621 nm=0.822 lgc - 0.187 (R2 = 0.990), and the LOD was 4.88 nM (S/N = 3). The construction of the dual-emission ratio FL sensing platform greatly eliminated and reduced the influence of potential interference factors, and improved the reliability of the experimental results. Compared with other H2S detection methods, the dual-mode sensor has an excellent detection range and a lower LOD as shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To test the selectivity of ECL and dual-emission FL sensor to the specific response to S2-, several common ions and ionic groups were selected as interfering substances, including Co2+, Cd2+, NH4+, Pb2+, Hg2+, NO3-, Mn2+, F -, Cl -, SO4 2-, S2O8 2-. 1 mM of the above ionic solution was prepared to obtain the corresponding ECL signal. Then add S2- of the same concentration to the reaction tank, and the signal quenching can be observed, as shown in Fig. 3(C). To verify the anti-interference capability of the FL sensor, prepared the above solution and S2- as 1 mM and obtained the output signals as shown in Fig. 4(C), the FL signal of the interferon ion hardly changes, which proved that the sensor had a specific response to S2-. The same Eu2O3@CDs /GCE ECL sensing platform was scanned continuously for 20 cycles for the signal stability test, and the results were shown in Fig. 3(D). Not only that, but it also verified the long-term stability of the same electrode as shown in Fig. S6(A), and the ECL performance test of five parallel modified electrodes as shown in Fig. S6(B). In addition, the long-term stability of the constructed FL sensor was tested as shown in Fig. 4(D). As a result, the dual-mode sensor showed excellent stability and reproducibility, laying a foundation for subsequent sustainable application and development.

Through the spiking test on the actual samples of lake water, tap water, and fish, for example, the recovery rate of the ECL sensor was between 86.49 % and 105.4 % as Table 1, and the recovery rate of the FL sensor was between 89.31 % and 118.6 % as Table 2. It can be proved that the established dual-mode sensing analysis method has high accuracy and good reproducibility and has important practical significance for monitoring H2S in environmental samples.

Table S2 presents the detection results obtained using the proposed methods alongside those obtained with the conventional method (ICP-MS) for S2- in tap water samples. The comparison demonstrates the accurate detection of S2- by both methods. Therefore, the proposed methods represent a viable approach for detecting S2- in environmental samples.

In this experiment, we developed the Eu2O3@CDs NPs composite materials based dual-mode sensing platforms for detection of H2S in environment. The Eu2O3@CDs NPs composite materials with excellent ECL stability and excellent dual-emission FL performance were synthesized simply and efficiently through the one-pot method. Because the identification unit S2- in H2S have the ability to destroy the FRET effect between Eu2O3 and CDs, causing the quenching of the ECL signal and the change of dual-emission ratio FL signal respectively. Our experimental results demonstrated that the ECL/FL dual-mode sensing platforms can provide rapidly quantitative response to S2- with low background, high sensitivity, low cost and easy manipulation, greatly eliminating the influence of uncontrollable factors such as test environment and testers, and significantly improving the accuracy and stability of experimental results. It provides a promising strategy for the sensitive detection of environmental pollutants and greatly expands the application prospects of eco-friendly, biological low toxicity CDs and rare earth materials.

Mingxia Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jie Zhou: Software, Methodology. Chunyuan Tian: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Feiyan Tang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xuming Zhuang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xidian Yue: Visualization, Validation, Software. Feng Luan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2023MB047, ZR2021MB024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136582.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chunyuan Tian obtained her Ph.D. degree in in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University in 2013, China. She is an associate professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University. Her research is focused on the preparation of new functional nanomaterial and electrochemiluminescence sensing application. E-mail: cytian@ytu.edu.cn

Feiyan Tang received her BS degree in science from Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, in 2016. She is studying for her master degree in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University. Her research interest covers electrochemical luminescence of rare earth nanomaterials. E-mail: tangfeiyan0807@163.com.

Mingxia Wang received her BS degree in science from Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Heze University, in 2022. She is studying for her master degree in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University. Her current research interest is electrochemiluminescence and fluorescence sensor for detection of metal ions and small biomolecules. E-mail: 19554530169@163.com

Jie Zhou is a freshman in applied chemistry at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University. She has learned about electrochemistry and hopes to further study. E-mail: 19806273151@163.com

Xidian Yue obtained his Ph.D. degree in food science from Ocean University of China in 2014, He is a lecturer in School of Life Sciences, Yantai University. His research is focused on the analysis and detection of functional and harmful components in food. E-mail: yuexidian@163.com

Feng Luan is a Professor of the Department of School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University. His research interests focus on computer-aided drug design and QSAR or QSPR studies of different chemical compounds, reaction dynamics and mechanisms, and lately electrochemical or biosensors applied to test environmental pollutants and biomolecules. E-mail: fluan@sina.com

Xuming Zhuang received his PhD in analytical chemistry from the Department of Chemistry of Shandong University, Jinan, in 2013. In the same year, he joined in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, as an associate professor. His current research interest focuses on preparation of novel sensors and biosensors of metal ions and small biomolecules, and applications to in vivo analysis. E-mail: xmzhuang@iccas.ac.cn
